skill.name: microsoft-teams-automation
skill.description: Automate Microsoft Teams workflows through Rube MCP, including messages, channels, chats, meetings, user lookup, and message search.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP to plan and run Microsoft Teams automation tasks with current tool schemas.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code for Rube and Microsoft Teams tool names. The real concern is that the skill routes Teams actions through a third-party MCP endpoint and OAuth connection, which can affect messages, meetings, members, and search results if permissions are granted.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Controls Microsoft Teams Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add an external Rube MCP endpoint and connect Microsoft Teams through OAuth. This is not confirmed malicious, but it can enable posting messages, creating chats, creating meetings, adding team members, and searching messages under the granted account permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly documents a third-party MCP endpoint plus OAuth setup and privileged Teams operations. The behavior is consistent with the skill purpose, so this is an elevated integration risk rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External MCP Endpoint
low_findings.description: The skill contains a hardcoded Rube MCP URL. This is expected for setup, but users should verify the endpoint and trust boundary before connecting a Microsoft tenant.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is directly present in the setup instructions. It is a legitimate service endpoint for this workflow, but it still creates a network trust dependency.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code spans for MCP tool names, Teams parameter names, and reference examples. No evidence of executable shell commands, scripts, or Ruby code was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation text and fenced Markdown examples. They do not invoke a local shell or interpolate user input into commands.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Findings Are Teams API Documentation
low_findings.description: The reported system and network reconnaissance patterns refer to Microsoft Teams listing, lookup, pagination, and search operations. These are normal Microsoft Graph workflow steps rather than local host or network scanning behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is Teams tenant object discovery needed for legitimate automation. No evidence found of local system probing, port scanning, or unrelated network reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged SaaS Automation Through External Connector
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill guides an assistant to use an external MCP connector for actions that can modify or disclose Microsoft Teams data. Users should review OAuth scopes and confirm tenant policy before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that different operations require Microsoft Graph permissions and admin consent may be needed. The risk depends on granted scopes and tenant controls.

content.value_statement: Teams automation often fails because IDs, permissions, pagination, and tool schemas change across tenants. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rube MCP workflows for messages, chats, meetings, channels, users, and search.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for Rube MCP and Microsoft Teams OAuth connection checks.
Explains how to resolve team, channel, chat, and user identifiers before actions.
Outlines workflows for posting channel messages and chat messages.
Describes meeting creation with participant and time validation requirements.
Documents Teams and channel management actions, including member operations.
Covers message search behavior, pagination, throttling, and common Graph errors.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP server and a Microsoft Teams connection.
Depends on Microsoft Graph permissions granted by the user or tenant administrator.
Does not provide executable scripts or standalone Teams API clients.
Search results may be delayed because Microsoft Teams search is eventually consistent.

use_cases.title: Post Operational Updates
use_cases.description: Send structured updates to the right Teams channel after resolving the team and channel identifiers.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Project Chats
use_cases.description: Create or reuse chats, find users, and send direct or group messages with correct member formats.

use_cases.title: Audit Teams Activity
use_cases.description: Search messages and list Teams resources while handling pagination, permissions, and delayed indexing.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Channel Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for posting one update.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Microsoft Teams automation skill to post this update to the correct Teams channel. First search for the current Rube tool schema, then resolve the team and channel IDs before sending.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Project Chat
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for direct or group chat setup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find these users in Microsoft Teams, create a group chat if needed, and send the kickoff message. Confirm the member format and include the authenticated user when required.

prompt_templates.title: Schedule a Teams Meeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Microsoft Teams meeting for these participants. Validate start and end times, resolve participant user IDs, and explain any permission issue before retrying.

prompt_templates.title: Search and Report Messages
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced task for investigation or reporting.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Microsoft Teams messages with this KQL query, handle pagination or delayed indexing, and summarize the relevant results without exposing unrelated message content.

output_examples.input: Post a release status update to the Engineering announcements channel.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the Engineering team, finds the announcements channel, confirms the message format, and reports that the update was posted.

output_examples.input: Create a Teams meeting tomorrow for the project steering group.
output_examples.output: The assistant resolves each participant, validates the meeting window, creates the online meeting, and returns the meeting subject and join details.

output_examples.input: Find messages about invoice approval from last week.
output_examples.output: The assistant builds a Teams search query, waits for available results when needed, and summarizes matching messages with dates and source context.

best_practices: Search Rube tools first so schemas and required parameters are current.
Resolve IDs from list operations instead of guessing Teams, channel, chat, or user identifiers.
Review permissions and tenant consent before enabling actions that send messages or manage members.
anti_patterns: Do not post messages before confirming the target team and channel IDs.
Do not assume email addresses can replace Microsoft Graph user IDs in every workflow.
Do not retry throttled operations rapidly; use backoff and respect Retry-After guidance.

faq.question: Does this skill require Microsoft Teams access?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires a Rube MCP connection and an active Microsoft Teams OAuth connection.

faq.question: Can it send Teams messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents workflows for channel messages and chat messages when permissions allow those actions.

faq.question: Can it create Teams meetings?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides meeting creation with subject, start time, end time, and participant user IDs.

faq.question: Does it manage Teams channels?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers listing teams, listing channels, creating channels, and reading team or channel details.

faq.question: Why must tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameters.

faq.question: Is this safe for enterprise tenants?
faq.answer: It can be used safely with tenant-approved OAuth scopes, administrator consent, and review of the external MCP endpoint.
